1→# Feature Specifications
     2→
     3→This directory contains detailed specifications for features in the Inventory APK project. Feature specifications provide clear, comprehensive documentation of requirements, design decisions, and implementation details for each major feature.
     4→
     5→## 📋 Purpose
     6→
     7→Feature specifications serve multiple critical purposes:
     8→
     9→1. **Clarity**: Establish shared understanding of what needs to be built
    10→2. **Planning**: Enable accurate effort estimation and milestone planning
    11→3. **Reference**: Provide developers with implementation guidance
    12→4. **Documentation**: Create historical record of feature decisions and rationale
    13→5. **Onboarding**: Help new contributors understand feature scope and architecture
    14→6. **Testing**: Define acceptance criteria and test scenarios
    15→
    16→## 🎯 When to Create a Feature Spec
    17→
    18→Create a feature specification document when:
    19→
    20→- ✅ Implementing a new user-facing feature
    21→- ✅ Adding significant new functionality to existing features
    22→- ✅ Making architectural changes that affect multiple components
    23→- ✅ Introducing new external dependencies or integrations
    24→- ✅ Planning features that require UI/UX design decisions
    25→- ✅ Features that take more than 1 week of development effort
    26→
    27→**Don't create specs for:**
    28→- ❌ Minor bug fixes
    29→- ❌ Simple UI tweaks or styling changes
    30→- ❌ Refactoring that doesn't change functionality
    31→- ❌ Documentation updates
    32→- ❌ Dependency version bumps
    33→
    34→## 📝 Feature Specification Process
    35→
    36→### 1. **Planning Phase**
    37→
    38→Before writing code, create a feature specification:
    39→
    40→```bash
    41→# Copy the template
    42→cp docs/features/TEMPLATE.md docs/features/[feature-name].md
    43→
    44→# Edit the new file with feature details
    45→# Feature name should use kebab-case (e.g., barcode-scanning.md)
    46→```
    47→
    48→### 2. **Writing the Spec**
    49→
    50→Follow the template structure (see `TEMPLATE.md`):
    51→
    52→- **Overview**: High-level description and goals
    53→- **User Stories**: Scenarios from user perspective
    54→- **Requirements**: Functional and non-functional requirements
    55→- **Technical Design**: Architecture, components, data models
    56→- **UI/UX Design**: Mockups, wireframes, user flows
    57→- **Implementation Plan**: Breaking down work into subtasks
    58→- **Testing Strategy**: How to verify the feature works
    59→- **Open Questions**: Unresolved decisions or blockers
    60→
    61→**Tips for writing good specs:**
    62→- Be specific and concrete, not vague or abstract
    63→- Include visual aids (diagrams, mockups, flowcharts)
    64→- Consider edge cases and error scenarios
    65→- Define acceptance criteria clearly
    66→- Link to related features or dependencies
    67→- Keep technical jargon to a minimum
    68→
    69→### 3. **Review & Approval**
    70→
    71→Before implementation begins:
    72→
    73→1. **Self-review**: Read through the spec critically
    74→2. **Team review**: Share with other developers for feedback
    75→3. **Revise**: Incorporate feedback and clarify ambiguities
    76→4. **Approve**: Get sign-off from project maintainer(s)
    77→5. **Link**: Add reference to the spec in the [ROADMAP.md](../../ROADMAP.md)
    78→
    79→### 4. **Implementation**
    80→
    81→During development:
    82→
    83→- Reference the spec for implementation details
    84→- Update the spec if requirements change (document why)
    85→- Mark sections as "✅ Implemented" when complete
    86→- Add notes about implementation decisions or deviations
    87→
    88→### 5. **Completion**
    89→
    90→After feature is shipped:
    91→
    92→- Update spec status to "Shipped" with version number
    93→- Link to pull requests and commits
    94→- Document any differences from original spec
    95→- Add lessons learned or notes for future work
    96→
    97→## 📂 File Naming Convention
    98→
    99→Feature specification files should follow this naming pattern:
   100→
   101→```
   102→[feature-name].md
   103→```
   104→
   105→**Examples:**
   106→- `item-management.md` - Core item CRUD operations
   107→- `barcode-scanning.md` - Barcode scanning functionality
   108→- `low-stock-alerts.md` - Notification system for low inventory
   109→- `csv-export.md` - Data export functionality
   110→- `multi-location-support.md` - Managing inventory across locations
   111→
   112→**Rules:**
   113→- Use lowercase
   114→- Separate words with hyphens (kebab-case)
   115→- Be descriptive but concise
   116→- Match feature name in roadmap when possible
   117→
   118→## 🗂️ Directory Structure
   119→
   120→```
   121→docs/features/
   122→├── README.md                    # This file - process documentation
   123→├── TEMPLATE.md                  # Template for new feature specs
   124→├── item-management.md           # Feature: Item CRUD operations
   125→├── barcode-scanning.md          # Feature: Barcode scanning
   126→├── category-management.md       # Feature: Categories and tags
   127→├── search-and-filter.md         # Feature: Search functionality
   128→└── ...                          # Additional feature specs
   129→```
   130→
   131→## 🔗 Relationship to Other Documentation
   132→
   133→Feature specifications complement other project documentation:
   134→
   135→| Document | Purpose | Relationship to Feature Specs |
   136→|----------|---------|-------------------------------|
   137→| **[README.md](../../README.md)** | Project overview | Lists high-level features; specs provide detail |
   138→| **[ROADMAP.md](../../ROADMAP.md)** | Development timeline | Specs are created for roadmap items as they're planned |
   139→| **[ARCHITECTURE.md](../ARCHITECTURE.md)** | Technical architecture | Specs reference architectural patterns and decisions |
   140→| **[CONTRIBUTING.md](../../CONTRIBUTING.md)** | Developer guidelines | Specs follow coding standards defined there |
   141→
   142→**Flow:**
   143→1. **ROADMAP.md** defines WHAT features will be built and WHEN
   144→2. **Feature Spec** defines HOW a feature will be built and WHY design decisions were made
   145→3. **ARCHITECTURE.md** defines overall patterns and principles
   146→4. **Pull Requests** reference the feature spec for context
   147→
   148→## ✅ Quality Checklist
   149→
   150→Before considering a feature spec complete, verify:
   151→
   152→- [ ] **Overview** clearly explains the feature's purpose and value
   153→- [ ] **User stories** describe real user scenarios
   154→- [ ] **Requirements** are specific and testable
   155→- [ ] **Technical design** includes architecture diagrams or data models
   156→- [ ] **UI/UX section** has mockups or wireframes (for UI features)
   157→- [ ] **Acceptance criteria** are clear and measurable
   158→- [ ] **Edge cases** and error scenarios are considered
   159→- [ ] **Dependencies** are identified and documented
   160→- [ ] **Testing strategy** explains how to verify the feature
   161→- [ ] **Open questions** are resolved or have a plan to resolve
   162→- [ ] Spec has been reviewed by at least one other person
   163→- [ ] Links to related docs (roadmap, architecture) are included
   164→
   165→## 📚 Examples and Templates
   166→
   167→### Example Feature Specs (To Be Created)
   168→
   169→As features are planned, examples will be added here:
   170→
   171→- **Item Management** - Core CRUD operations (MVP)
   172→- **Barcode Scanning** - Camera integration and barcode detection (MVP)
   173→- **Category System** - Organizing items by category (MVP)
   174→- **Low Stock Alerts** - Notification system (MVP)
   175→
   176→### Using the Template
   177→
   178→```bash
   179→# Create a new feature spec
   180→cp docs/features/TEMPLATE.md docs/features/my-new-feature.md
   181→
   182→# Edit the file and fill in all sections
   183→# Remove any sections that don't apply (mark as "N/A" if unsure)
   184→# Add additional sections if needed for your specific feature
   185→```
   186→
   187→## 🔄 Updating Feature Specs
   188→
   189→Feature specs are living documents during development:
   190→
   191→**When to update:**
   192→- Requirements change based on new information
   193→- Technical approach shifts during implementation
   194→- New edge cases or scenarios are discovered
   195→- UI/UX design is refined
   196→- Dependencies or timelines change
   197→
   198→**How to update:**
   199→1. Make changes directly in the spec file
   200→2. Add a note in the "Change Log" section explaining what changed and why
   201→3. Update the "Last Updated" date
   202→4. If changes are significant, notify the team
   203→5. Commit changes with a descriptive message
   204→
   205→**Change Log Format:**
   206→```markdown
   207→## Change Log
   208→
   209→### 2026-03-25
   210→- Changed database schema to use UUID instead of auto-increment ID
   211→- Reason: Enables future cloud sync without ID conflicts
   212→
   213→### 2026-03-20
   214→- Added support for item images
   215→- Reason: User feedback indicated this was a high-priority feature
   216→```
   217→
   218→## 🤝 Contributing
   219→
   220→When contributing feature specifications:
   221→
   222→1. **Use the template**: Start with `TEMPLATE.md`
   223→2. **Be thorough**: Complete all sections with detail
   224→3. **Include visuals**: Add diagrams, mockups, or screenshots
   225→4. **Consider the user**: Write user stories from their perspective
   226→5. **Think ahead**: Document edge cases and future considerations
   227→6. **Get feedback**: Have others review before implementation
   228→7. **Keep it updated**: Modify the spec as implementation progresses
   229→
   230→## 📧 Questions?
   231→
   232→If you have questions about the feature specification process:
   233→
   234→- Review the [CONTRIBUTING.md](../../CONTRIBUTING.md) guidelines
   235→- Check existing feature specs for examples
   236→- Open a GitHub Discussion for process questions
   237→- Tag maintainers in GitHub Issues for clarification
